elements.sent-2411-001: Stochastic diffusion equations are crucial for modeling a range of physical phenomena influenced by uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-002: We introduce the generalized finite difference method for solving these equations.
elements.sent-2411-003: Then, we examine its consistency, stability and convergence in mean-square, showing that the proposed method preserves stability and demonstrates favorable convergence characteristics under suitable assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In order to validate the methodology, we present numerical results in one-, two-, and three-dimensional space domains.
elements.sent-2411-005: Dynamical systems in science and engineering are most commonly modeled using ordinary differential equations (ODEs).
elements.sent-2411-006: Real-world phenomena often involve inherent uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-007: We can describe these uncertainties using random processes, which allows the incorporation of randomness into ODE models, leading to the concept of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).
elements.sent-2411-008: Several numerical methods have been developed for solving SDEs, see e.g. \cite{Kloeden1992,Buckwar2007,Shahmoradi2021,Li2023,sde2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: SDEs describe the evolution of random processes over time, whereas stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) expand this framework to include spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-010: SPDEs have been extensively employed to model various applications in mathematical sciences, physics, biology and engineering.
elements.sent-2411-011: There exists an enormous literature on solving SPDEs, see e.g. \cite{Cui2017,Anton2020,Chen2020,Jentzen2020,Yang2021,Zhao2020,Nassajian2024,Jentzen2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: One of the oldest and simplest methods used for solving flow and heat transfer problems, is the finite difference method \cite{Courant1928 , particularly when the domain is not complex.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, this classic method remained limited by the necessity of using regular meshes.
elements.sent-2411-014: In \cite{Liszka1980 has been developed a finite difference on irregular regions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Then, in \cite{Benito2001 an explicit form of the meshless finite difference method, called the generalized finite difference method, was presented.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The generalized finite difference method derived from the finite difference method, enables its application to scattered points and irregular areas.
elements.sent-2411-017: This method is based on Taylor expansion and the moving least squares method.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this method, partial derivatives are approximated using a combination of function values and weighting coefficients at each nodal point and its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-019: Due to the characteristics of meshless methods, this approach is highly flexible for solving partial differential equations and can be easily applied to irregular domains.
elements.sent-2411-020: That is why in recent years it has been utilized to address various numerical problems across different scientific disciplines, for example, \cite{Fan2014,Li2017,Fu2019,Li2021,Izadian2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: Firstly, the stochastic generalized finite difference scheme is introduced in Section \ref{sec:methodology .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section \ref{sec:stability , we analyze the consistency, stability and convergence of the proposed method.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Numerical examples are presented in Section \ref{sec:numerics .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In Section \ref{sec:conclusion , we discuss our results, and outline potential directions for future research.}
